comprehensive_summary:
Johann Wolfgang von Goethe is a German polymath and one of the most prominent figures in quizbowl literature, clued across an enormous range of works spanning poetry, drama, novels, and scientific treatises. His two-part play Faust is his most iconic work: in it, the title scholar makes a bet with Mephistopheles that he will not tell the passing moment 'tarry awhile, thou art so fair.' Faust dances with witches on Walpurgis Night, encounters a homunculus, has a child with Helen of Troy, and the play ends by invoking the 'eternal feminine' that 'draws us on high.' Gretchen, the female lead, sings 'The King in Thule' and laments 'My peace is gone, my heart is sore' at her spinning wheel, the latter inspiring Schubert's famous lied 'Gretchen am Spinnrade.' The prologue includes scenes set both in a theater and in Heaven. Faust Part II features Simon Magus appearing on paper money. Delacroix created lithographs illustrating Faust in 1828, Harry Clarke made Art Nouveau illustrations in 1925, and Charles Gounod composed an opera based on it.

Goethe's epistolary novel The Sorrows of Young Werther is his other most-clued work and a quintessential Sturm und Drang text. The protagonist Werther falls in love with Charlotte (Lotte) and cries when she says the name 'Klopstock.' After Lotte marries Albert, Werther borrows Albert's pistols and commits suicide; he is buried under a linden tree. A contemporary (Friedrich Nicolai) parodied the novel by adding chicken's blood to the suicide scene. Thomas Mann's novel Lotte in Weimar depicts Goethe's childhood lover Charlotte confronting him for fictionalizing her.

Goethe's ballad 'Der Erlkonig' is one of the most frequently clued individual poems. It opens 'Who rides so late through the night and wind?' and depicts a father riding on horseback with his son, who is tempted by the Erlking's promises that 'my daughters shall tend thee with sisterly care.' The father dismisses the apparitions as willows and fog, but the child dies in his father's arms. Schubert's famous setting opens with G octaves in the piano's right hand.

Goethe's Italian sojourn (1786-88) produced multiple works: the Roman Elegies (originally titled Erotica, dedicated to Christiane Vulpius), Venetian Epigrams, and the travelogue Italian Journey with the epigraph 'I, too in Arcadia.' The first Roman Elegy commands 'Tell me you stones, O speak, you towering palaces!' The fourth Roman Elegy imagines people questioning him about Werther. J.H.W. Tischbein painted Goethe reclining on a rock slab 'in the Roman Campagna' next to a relief of Iphigenia's sacrifice during this trip. Angelica Kauffman also painted a portrait of Goethe in Rome.

The West-Eastern Divan was inspired by Joseph Hammer-Purgstall's translation of Hafez and an affair with Marianne von Willemer. It uses ghazals and features conversations between the lovers Hatem and Suleika. Each of its twelve books has a Persian and German title. The Book of the Singer opens the collection.

Elective Affinities is a novel in which Eduard and his wife Charlotte invite Captain Otto and Ottilie to their Weimar home, and the resulting romantic attractions are explained through the metaphor of chemical reactions (double-displacement). A woman loses her footing while pushing a rowboat into a lake and drops her baby cousin, causing him to drown. Walter Benjamin wrote an essay on it.

The Wilhelm Meister novels are Goethe's major bildungsroman series. In Wilhelm Meister's Apprenticeship, the protagonist joins an acting troupe, plays Hamlet, is rejected by Mariane, and eventually joins the Tower Society. The character Mignon sings 'Do you know the land where the lemon trees bloom?' and 'None but the Lonely Heart.' Goethe's last novel details Wilhelm's 'Journeyman Years.' Friedrich Schlegel adopted the concept of 'Bildung' partly from this work.

Goethe wrote two poems titled 'Wanderer's Nightsong.' One declares 'Over all hilltops is peace' and notes that 'in all the treetops / hearest thou / hardly a breath,' ending with 'soon thou too shalt rest.' He inscribed the lines of the second on a cabin wall. 'The Sorcerer's Apprentice' features a magic broom instructed to fetch pails of water that floods a workshop; Paul Dukas set it to music. Goethe also wrote 'Welcome and Farewell,' 'Blessed Longing' (about a moth consumed by a flame), 'The God and the Bayadere,' 'Anacreon's Grave,' and a poem harshly criticizing Christianity titled 'Prometheus.'

The 'Marienbad Elegy' was written after the 73-year-old Goethe proposed to 17-year-old Ulrike von Levetzow and was rejected. It includes the lines 'They urged me to those lips, with rapture crowned / Deserted me, and hurled me to the ground.' It forms the second part of a 'Trilogy of Passion.'

Goethe's scientific works include Theory of Colors, an anti-Newtonian treatise that influenced a triangle of nine segments with symbolic labels like 'unnecessary.' Turner referenced it in a painting about the morning after the biblical flood. Ludwig Wittgenstein's Remarks on Colour expands on ideas from it. The Metamorphosis of Plants drew on Goethe's theory of a primordial 'Urpflanze' from which all plant life developed.

Biographically, Goethe was a major exponent of the Sturm und Drang movement, was a childhood friend of Friedrich Klinger, worked for the Duke of Saxe-Weimar who ennobled him, was regarded as the second-most famous European behind Napoleon in his lifetime, maintained skepticism against German nationalism, met Napoleon (who called his translation of Voltaire's Mahomet 'an awful play'), called out 'more light! more light!' on his deathbed, and was a pioneer of the Innerlichkeit style. Hugo Wolf wrote 51 art songs based on his texts. Thomas Mann was deeply influenced by Goethe, writing about him in the essay 'Goethe as a Representative of the Bourgeois Age,' the novel Lotte in Weimar, and modeling Doktor Faustus on Faust. Goethe coined the phrase 'the epoch of world literature is at hand' and defined a novella as telling of a 'single remarkable event.' His table talk was recorded by his secretary Eckermann.

works.name: Faust
works.description: A two-part play in which the title scholar makes a deal with Mephistopheles. Part I centers on Faust's relationship with Gretchen; Part II features encounters with Helen of Troy and a homunculus. The play includes a prologue in Heaven and concludes by invoking the 'eternal feminine.'

clues.clue: Faust bets Mephistopheles he will not tell the passing moment 'tarry awhile, thou art so fair'
quotes: the title character makes a bet that he will not tell the passing moment 'tarry awhile, thou art so fair!'
Faust agrees to become Mephistopheles's servant in exchange for a moment that makes him say 'Ah, stay a while! You are so lovely'

clues.clue: Gretchen sings 'My peace is gone, my heart is sore' at her spinning wheel (basis for Schubert's 'Gretchen am Spinnrade')
quotes: an ostinato on the sixteenth notes 'F, A, F, E, D, E' underlies a piece that sets a text... whose verses all begin, 'My peace is gone / My heart is heavy'
Schubert used a 'spinning' motif in the lied 'Gretchen at the Spinning Wheel'

quotes: The final lines of a play by this poet invoke an 'eternal feminine' that 'draws us on high'

quotes: In 1828, Eugene Delacroix published a series of lithographs illustrating this writer's best known work

quotes: 'Anacreon's Grave' is one of the texts by this author that are set in a cycle of 51 songs by Hugo Wolf, which opens with three settings of this author's 'The Harp Player'

works.name: Der Erlkonig
works.description: A ballad in which a father rides through the night with his son, who is tempted by the Erlking. The father dismisses the son's fears, but the child dies in his father's arms. Famously set to music by Schubert.

works.name: Italian Journey / Roman Elegies / Venetian Epigrams
works.description: Works produced from Goethe's 1786-88 travels in Italy. The Roman Elegies are a cycle of 24 sensual poems (originally titled Erotica) dedicated to Christiane Vulpius. The Venetian Epigrams are a separate collection. Italian Journey is a travelogue with the epigraph 'I, too in Arcadia.'

clues.clue: The first Roman Elegy commands 'Tell me you stones, O speak, you towering palaces!'
quotes: This author commanded, 'Tell me you stones, O speak, you towering palaces!' to open the first poem in a 24-poem cycle
a poem whose speaker commands: 'Tell me you stones, O speak, you towering palaces! / Streets, say a word'

clues.clue: Roman Elegies originally titled Erotica, dedicated to Christiane Vulpius, inspired by Italian art seen 1786-88
quotes: this author wrote a cycle of twenty-four sensual poems originally titled Erotica, that were inspired by the classical art he saw during his travels from 1786-88

clues.clue: Tischbein painted Goethe 'in the Roman Campagna' reclining on a rock slab next to a relief of Iphigenia's sacrifice
quotes: Johann Tischbein painted this man 'in the Roman Campagna' during a trip this man took to Italy

works.name: Wilhelm Meister novels

works.name: Theory of Colors / Scientific Works
works.description: Goethe's anti-Newtonian treatise on color theory, and his botanical work The Metamorphosis of Plants which posited a primordial 'Urpflanze.'

Play: Mahler's Symphony of a Thousand uses texts from Part II for the holy anchorites sung by chorus
Novel: the protagonist cries when the woman he loves says the name 'Klopstock'
cards.back: The Sorrows of Young Werther (Johann Wolfgang von Goethe)

cards.front: Novel: the protagonist bonds with the woman he loves over their mutual appreciation of Goldsmith and Klopstock
cards.back: The Sorrows of Young Werther (Johann Wolfgang von Goethe)

cards.front: Novel: Friedrich Nicolai parodied this work by adding chicken's blood to the suicide scene
cards.back: The Sorrows of Young Werther (Johann Wolfgang von Goethe)

cards.front: Novel: the protagonist receives a pink ribbon in the mail
cards.back: The Sorrows of Young Werther (Johann Wolfgang von Goethe)

cards.front: Poem: the title figure promises 'my daughters shall tend thee with sisterly care' to a boy riding on horseback with his father
Poem: the father dismisses his son's fears by saying ''tis the aged grey willows deceiving thy sight' and 'it's a streak of fog'
Poem: Schubert's setting opens with G octaves in the piano's right hand
Work: the first poem commands 'Tell me you stones, O speak, you towering palaces!' in a cycle of 24 sensual poems originally titled Erotica
cards.work: Italian Journey / Roman Elegies / Venetian Epigrams

cards.front: Work: Tischbein painted the author reclining on a rock slab 'in the Roman Campagna' next to a relief of Iphigenia's sacrifice
cards.work: Italian Journey / Roman Elegies / Venetian Epigrams

cards.front: Work: a travelogue with the epigraph 'I, too in Arcadia,' recounting a 1786-88 journey
cards.work: Italian Journey / Roman Elegies / Venetian Epigrams

cards.front: Work: the fourth poem imagines people questioning the author about a suicidal character from another novel
cards.work: Italian Journey / Roman Elegies / Venetian Epigrams

cards.front: Work: dedicated to Christiane Vulpius; Angelica Kauffman painted a portrait of the author in Rome during this period
cards.work: Italian Journey / Roman Elegies / Venetian Epigrams

cards.front: Work: inspired by Joseph Hammer-Purgstall's translation of Hafez and an affair with Marianne von Willemer; uses ghazals
Work: features conversations between the lovers Hatem and Suleika; each of its twelve books has a Persian and German title
Novel: the romantic attractions between two couples are explained through chemical double-displacement reactions; Captain Otto and Ottilie are invited to a Weimar home
Novel: a woman drops her baby cousin while pushing a rowboat into a lake, causing the baby to drown
Novel: inspired by an aphorism about people who 'mix like water and wine' or 'separate like water and oil'; Walter Benjamin wrote an essay on it
Novel: the protagonist plays Hamlet in an acting troupe after being rejected by Mariane, and eventually joins the Tower Society
cards.back: Wilhelm Meister's Apprenticeship (Johann Wolfgang von Goethe)

cards.front: Novel: the character Mignon sings 'Do you know the land where the lemon trees bloom?'
cards.back: Wilhelm Meister's Apprenticeship (Johann Wolfgang von Goethe)

cards.front: Novel: Mignon also sings 'None but the Lonely Heart' ('Nur wer die Sehnsucht kennt')
cards.back: Wilhelm Meister's Apprenticeship (Johann Wolfgang von Goethe)

cards.front: Novel: Mignon is first introduced as a child purchased from a theatre troupe; she later performs an egg dance
cards.back: Wilhelm Meister's Apprenticeship (Johann Wolfgang von Goethe)

cards.front: Poem: 'Over all hilltops is peace... in all the treetops / hearest thou / hardly a breath'; ends with 'soon thou too shalt rest'
Poem: the author inscribed the lines of the second poem on the walls of a cabin
Poem: written after a 73-year-old author proposed to 17-year-old Ulrike von Levetzow and was rejected; forms the second part of a 'Trilogy of Passion'
Poem: contains the lines 'They urged me to those lips, with rapture crowned / Deserted me, and hurled me to the ground'
Poem: a magic broom is instructed to fetch pails of water and floods a workshop; the apprentice futilely cuts the broom in half
cards.back: The Sorcerer's Apprentice (Johann Wolfgang von Goethe)

cards.front: Poem: source of the idiom 'the spirits that I called'; set to music by Paul Dukas
cards.back: The Sorcerer's Apprentice (Johann Wolfgang von Goethe)

cards.front: Work: an anti-Newtonian treatise; associated with a triangle of nine segments using labels like 'unnecessary'
cards.back: Theory of Colors (Johann Wolfgang von Goethe)

cards.front: Work: Wittgenstein's Remarks on Colour expands on ideas from this treatise; Turner referenced it in a painting about the morning after the flood
cards.back: Theory of Colors (Johann Wolfgang von Goethe)

cards.front: Work: posits a primordial 'Urpflanze' from which all plant life developed
cards.back: The Metamorphosis of Plants (Johann Wolfgang von Goethe)

cards.front: Poem: a moth consumed by a flame; speaker writes 'You remain no longer caught / In the penumbral gloom'
Poem: a temple prostitute is deified for committing sati after discovering an avatar of Shiva in her bed
cards.back: The God and the Bayadere (Johann Wolfgang von Goethe)

cards.front: Poem: ends with 'yet, to be loved, what happiness! / What happiness, ye gods, to love!' after describing a horseback ride to a nighttime tryst
cards.back: Welcome and Farewell (Johann Wolfgang von Goethe)

cards.front: Poem: a boy picks a rosebud despite its promise to prick him
Play: ends with Freedom visiting Klarchen's lover in jail; Beethoven composed the overture
Author: called out 'more light! more light!' on his deathbed
Author: secretary Eckermann recorded nine years of his table talk; he quipped 'architecture is frozen music' and advanced a notion of the 'Daemonic'
Author: declared 'the classical I call healthy, and the romantic sickly'
Author: coined the phrase 'the epoch of world literature is at hand' while comparing a Chinese novel to one of his own works
Author: defined a novella as telling of a 'single remarkable event'
Author: during an interview with Napoleon, Pierre Daru mentioned his translation of Voltaire's Mahomet, and Napoleon responded 'That is an awful play'
Author: first published Diderot's Rameau's Nephew in a German translation
Author: a pioneer of the Innerlichkeit style; worked for the Duke of Saxe-Weimar, who ennobled him
Author: wrote the story 'The Green Snake and the Beautiful Lily,' informed by an interest in alchemy
Author: a section of Nabokov's 'Pale Fire' quotes him after the line 'And now what shall I do? My knight is pinned'

summary: Johann Wolfgang von Goethe is a German polymath and one of the most prominent figures in quizbowl literature, clued across an enormous range of works spanning poetry, drama, novels, and scientific treatises.
